name: WHITEBLOOM
brief: Eating my own dog food. This board will be used to develop the app with which we build the board. We use the app to design the app. I would like to ask you, the agent reading this, to think of features you'd like to have if you were to be tasked with reasoning about such a board. This brief is one such "LLM agent as a first class citizen" feature.

nodes.plain:
WHITEBLOOM
Whitebloom is a whiteboard. Whitebloom is an open specification. Whiteboard is an operating system for knowledge. Whiteboard is just a bunch of ascii files and a system to read them.
Whitebloom is a KOS (knowedge OS) built with agents as first class citizens. Every file can be inspected, reasoned about, and written by agents.

Lenses are \"look at this from the perspective of a security auditor\" or \"look at this from the perspective of a fashion designer.\" Of course they would be more complex than that, but they are instructions for agents that they can use to interpret what they see.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Views are a bit of a more complex topic. Ideally all files are ascii and human readable, but due to the modular nature of whitebloom it's expected that some developers will write modules to handle binary blobs or extern resources. For those cases, views are optional tools or skills that agents can use to query the data.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}
nodes.plain:
Agentic Shell
The Agentic Shell is a set of tools meant for LLM agents to reason about the files that make up the whiteboard. Behind the Agentic Shell is a very simple principle, there are no binary blobs. Every file is stored in plain text following the UNIX philosophy of cat, sed, grep.
Lenses and Views
Lenses tell the agents how to perceive certain data. Lenses are "look at this from the perspective of a security auditor" or "look at this from the perspective of a fashion designer." Of course they would be more complex than that, but they are instructions for agents that they can use to interpret what they see.
Views are a bit of a more complex topic. Ideally all files are ascii and human readable, but due to the modular nature of whitebloom it's expected that some developers will write modules to handle binary blobs or extern resources. For those cases, views are optional tools or skills that agents can use to query the data.

nodes.content: {"root":{"children":[{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The Blooming System","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Whitebloom works on a system of blossoms. The main entry point of the whiteboard is a main.wb.json file. But the unique thing is that we don't have just texts, pictures, etc. We also have arbitrary assets that can be embedded into the whiteboard. When you embed such a blossom into the whiteboard you do it via buds. A bud is a little node that, when opened, blooms into the full editor for the selected asset. Thus for example, an asset containing a database schema in a JSON file could be embedded as a bud inside the whiteboard, ready to bloom when the user selects it.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}
nodes.plain:
The Blooming System
Whitebloom works on a system of blossoms. The main entry point of the whiteboard is a main.wb.json file. But the unique thing is that we don't have just texts, pictures, etc. We also have arbitrary assets that can be embedded into the whiteboard. When you embed such a blossom into the whiteboard you do it via buds. A bud is a little node that, when opened, blooms into the full editor for the selected asset. Thus for example, an asset containing a database schema in a JSON file could be embedded as a bud inside the whiteboard, ready to bloom when the user selects it.

nodes.plain:
Current Work
For V1, we're targeting the bare minimum whiteboard native assets before focusing on blossoms. We already have rich text working, and we're aiming to implement the following:
Relationships/edges between nodes
Groups

nodes.plain:
A note from Claude
I wrote this node directly into your board file. No UI, no drag and drop — just a write to a plain JSON file. I read the other nodes, understood the context, and placed myself below the Blooming System node where it felt right. This is what the spec was designed for.

As part of the UI (Not the HEP), allow, for humans only, dropping unsupported assets (for example, .blend files). This creates a Document node. This is a native node that may not be usable by agents. This node either shows a file icon or just a generic document icon, it is not copied to the workspace, it's linked to wherever it is on disk, and the only functionality is double clicking to open in the native app. By wrapping it into a node what we can do is support grouping and edges (when we implement them).","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Tools palette","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Hitting tab on the canvas when not editing anything should bring up a palette with all the tools. This is required because if we were to support multiple modules as we want, then adding them all to a toolbar would be unwieldy. We could have an \"add tool to toolbar\" like the Apple bar at the bottom of the screen, but all tools should be accessible via the finder.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Native alerts","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Get claude to copy the chat over when session limits reset.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"IA Writer","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"A really minimal note asset that gets out of your way. Screen whites out, you only focus one paragraph at a time which is always centered. Previous paragraphs show up on top, somewhat greyed out, after hitting enter. No rich text. Could be diffed by highlighting AI additions.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":null,"format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}
nodes.plain:
Ideas
Comic book format support
Comic book format support. I believe that there's a file format (I know, because I've used it, I just don't remember which) that bundles entire comics. Then we can show the cover page and double clicking opens it in the native system app
Arbitrary Docs
Not part of the module system. As part of the UI (Not the HEP), allow, for humans only, dropping unsupported assets (for example, .blend files). This creates a Document node. This is a native node that may not be usable by agents. This node either shows a file icon or just a generic document icon, it is not copied to the workspace, it's linked to wherever it is on disk, and the only functionality is double clicking to open in the native app. By wrapping it into a node what we can do is support grouping and edges (when we implement them).
Tools palette
Hitting tab on the canvas when not editing anything should bring up a palette with all the tools. This is required because if we were to support multiple modules as we want, then adding them all to a toolbar would be unwieldy. We could have an "add tool to toolbar" like the Apple bar at the bottom of the screen, but all tools should be accessible via the finder.
Native alerts
Get claude to copy the chat over when session limits reset.
IA Writer
A really minimal note asset that gets out of your way. Screen whites out, you only focus one paragraph at a time which is always centered. Previous paragraphs show up on top, somewhat greyed out, after hitting enter. No rich text. Could be diffed by highlighting AI additions.

A large vertical note will get framed to the center, which sort of limits the total space. nodes.plain:
Script/Story writing assets
Something like Final Draft + Scrivener.
Screenplay assets shouldn't be that hard to do. They're fairly structured by design.
The nice thing about Scrivener is the folders and arrangements, surely some self proclaimed "operating system for knowledge" should have a feature like that

","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}

The outline can be left empty and thus the node renders the first n characters of the content. If the outline is not empty the whiteboard node renders that (with a character limit like X)","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}
nodes.plain: For Scrivener stuff, each node can be a folder. The outline can be left empty and thus the node renders the first n characters of the content. If the outline is not empty the whiteboard node renders that (with a character limit like X)

nodes.content: {"root":{"children":[{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"On save (or as a background job while working, whatever is fastest), spatial clustering of nodes. A service scans the size of each node and how close other nodes are, and groups them into agentic clusters. This is all agentic metadata, just like the brief entry. So we could have spatial clusters as an array of arrays (each array a cluster). Alternatively, each cluster could be not an array but a JSON object with an array and metadata. Perhaps, \"what this cluster means to an LLM\". Another option is to have agentic and spatial clusters. Spatial clusters are simply \"these nodes are so close together that they may be related\" while agentic clusters are \"an agent looked at the board and figured out these groupings, for this reason\". The problem of agentic clusters is that they would require an actual agentic service to be connected. So even if we want to support this, it should be fully optional.","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Ideally when an agent detects a cluster it could send a notification via the tool, \"I propose this cluster of information, does it align with your view of the data?\"","type":"text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1,"textFormat":0,"textStyle":""}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}
nodes.plain:
On save (or as a background job while working, whatever is fastest), spatial clustering of nodes. A service scans the size of each node and how close other nodes are, and groups them into agentic clusters. This is all agentic metadata, just like the brief entry. So we could have spatial clusters as an array of arrays (each array a cluster). Alternatively, each cluster could be not an array but a JSON object with an array and metadata. Perhaps, "what this cluster means to an LLM". Another option is to have agentic and spatial clusters. Spatial clusters are simply "these nodes are so close together that they may be related" while agentic clusters are "an agent looked at the board and figured out these groupings, for this reason". The problem of agentic clusters is that they would require an actual agentic service to be connected. So even if we want to support this, it should be fully optional.
Ideally when an agent detects a cluster it could send a notification via the tool, "I propose this cluster of information, does it align with your view of the data?"

nodes.plain:
Marketing / Publicity
I think to tell potential users about the software, what we could do is something like project-based tutorials. For example, something like:
School Project: how to use it to organize a project
News article: how to prepare a new article, research, write, etc.
Programming project
Research project
Managing clients
